title_tesim: "Die Fledermaus" production, Symphonies by the Sea, Santa Monica, 1965
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Negs. Fledermaus. 8/27/65 for #2 paper.
named_subject_tesim: Symphonies by the Sea (Santa Monica, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Event noted in the article, "Symphony-by-the-Sea Programs Scheduled,"  a Los Angeles Times, 11 July 1965: WS7.
Symphonies by the Sea was a free-admission annual symphonic and operatic season which started at Santa Monica High School, then moved on to Santa Monica City College.

An opera singer, possibly portraying Rosalind, performs at center stage in Act 2 of Die Fledermaus, while fellow performers (wearing late-19th century ball gowns and tuxedos) watch her. Set props include garden tables, benches, and banquettes; a pair of lanterns is viewed glowing in the background. Two accompanying pianists can be seen beyond the stage at middle ground. Performed August 27, 1965, during the 28th annual “Symphonies by the Sea” event, Open-Air Memorial Theater, Santa Monica High School Campus.
combined_subject_ssim: Symphonies by the Sea (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: Santa Monica College students in an outdoor theatre production, Santa Monica, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2948. 5/15/37. Santa Monica Junior College. Booklet. Music. Hold. See other proofing file. 1953.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Young men and women in an outdoor theatre production. Most of the women on stage wear dirndls; one woman at center wears a straw bonnet. Three males wear 17th century attire: colonial/pilgrim-type shoes, breaches and doublets along with flat, pointed collared shirts and capes. Two of the three men are interacting with two of the women, while the remaining actors point in their direction. A man dressed as a magistrate sits in the foreground on the left. Above them, two microphones are strung up with wire running the width of the stage. The orchestra plays at ground level while three women sit on an piano bench facing the conductor. Spectators can be viewed seated on benches in the foreground.

title_tesim: Santa Monica College students using surveying equipment, Santa Monica, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2948. 5/15/37. Santa Monica Junior College. Booklet. Music. Hold. See other proofing file. 1953.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Three male students, wearing 1930s era clothing, stand outdoors using surveying and mapping equipment. One stands at a tripod mounted with an engineer’s transit. A second male stands at an elevated drawing table with pencil in hand, a second transit placed in front of him. The third male stands in the background holding a surveyors measuring stick. A gated area, trees, parked cars and a lamppost can be seen in the background.

title_tesim: Santa Monica College students outdoors, Santa Monica, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2948. 5/15/37. Santa Monica Junior College. Booklet. Music. Hold. See other proofing file. 1953.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Young women seated and standing on a brick patio situated between two buildings. There are nine women in total. The one closest to the camera sits on a step and looks off to her left. Just beyond her, three women appear to be studying at a picnic table; a woman sits in a reclining chair behind them looking at the camera as does a second woman seated at adjacent table. Two others have a conversation behind her while a girl watches. The patio is replete with trees and planted flowers.

title_tesim: Santa Monica College student sculptor, Santa Monica, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2948. 5/15/37. Santa Monica Junior College. Booklet. Music. Hold. See other proofing file. 1953.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: A young woman creates a relief sculpture. The woman, wearing 1930s era blouse, sweater vest and skirt, sits at a table facing a wall with wainscot paneling; three prints illustrating design examples are pinned to the wall. A bust of a female sits on the tables where the student carves what appears to be a Madonna image from plaster. A three-sided ruler and chisel are visible on the right.

title_tesim: Façade of Santa Monica High School, Santa Monica, 1939
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Santa Monica High. Views of Campus 2/1/39. Steffy Pierson, the misses.
named_subject_tesim: Santa Monica High School (Santa Monica, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of Santa Monica High School façade as seen from a palm tree-lined pathway. The path (with intermittent sets of stairs) runs parallel to the two-story Moderne-style building lined with windows. The stairs turn and lead up a manicured lawn to a portico with the school’s name carved over it.
Portions of Santa Monica High School were destroyed during the earthquake of 1933. Its reconstruction fell under the Works Project Administration (WPA). The project was completed in 1939.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica High School (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: Clipping regarding Symphonies by the Sea, letter by Adelbert Bartlett, 1965
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Negs. Fledermaus. 8/27/65 for #2 paper.
named_subject_tesim: Symphonies by the Sea (Santa Monica, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Symphonies by the Sea was a free-admission annual symphonic and operatic season which started at Santa Monica High School, then moved on to Santa Monica City College.

Newspaper clipping featuring a letter to the editor of the Evening Outlook, written by Adelbert Bartlett, published September 6, 1965. In it, he thanks the city of Santa Monica, the Board of Education, and Music Arts Society for their cooperation during the season of Symphonies by the Sea. The letter describes the season's finale of "Die Fledermaus" as a "triumph," and notes that some 3000 people attended the event.
combined_subject_ssim: Symphonies by the Sea (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: Judy Mahood, Alicia Mowat, and Carolyn Crane, ballet students, in costume, 1956
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Julia Stuart. Oriental. Alicia.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of Judy Mahood (left), Alicia Mowat (center), and Carolyn Crane, ballet students, in oriental costume at a ballet rehearsal or performance. They were students of Julia Stuart.
Information about Alicia Mowat, Judy Mahood and Carolyn Crane as ballet students is provided by newspaper clippings photographed by Bartlett (image ark no. 21198/zz002d5hdr).

As a teenager Alicia Mowat won several beauty pageants in the California (Miss Hollywood, Miss Reseda, Miss Malibu, Miss Huntington Beach, and others). In the 1960's she acted in several Italian films under the name Alicia Brandet (including I ragazzi dell’hully-gully (1964) and Una storia d’amore (1970). She married Giancarl Marcaccini in 1970.

description_tesim: Choreography by Julia Stuart. The gingerbread children were portrayed by members of University High School’s a capella choir, as noted in a newspaper write-up with title, “To Sing in Light Opera.” Article is one of several assembled by Adelbert Bartlett referring to this production. (see "Relation" note).

Santa Monica Civic Opera production of “Hansel and Gretel.” Cast members line up across the stage, presumably at performance end. The stage set depicts a cottage in the woods. Primary cast members include, from left: Catherine Mazet as the sandman, Barbara Ludwig as Gretel, June Moss as the mother, Teresa Kirbe as the witch, Lyle Heck as father, Charlie Marie Gordon as Hansel, and Marlene Owen as the dew fairy (far right) with additional cast of gingerbread children and dancers. Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.
Photograph used for poster mock-up [image ark no. 21198/zz002d5ckd]

For additional related photos, see "Relation" note.

Typescript document, with corrections, describing two photographs. The descriptions are similar except that the caption at top describes three women – Jane Riley, Naida Sparks and Dorothy Dittrick. The bottom caption omits Dittrick. Text reads, in part: [Pencil:] 2702. [Typewritten:] These three smiling “Blossoms,” charter members of the Exalted Order of the Pumpkin Vine, thriving women’s good time’s group at Santa Monica Junior College, go on their annual harvest-time pilgrimage to gather the popular pie-filler and to practice the age-old art of corn-shucking at the municipal vegetable gardens on Ocean Park Heights where hundreds of unemployed citizens grow patches of succulent foodstuffs. In the picture, from left to right, are the Misses Jane Riley, Naida Sparks, and Dorothy Dittrick, popular Samojac c-oeds [sic].

title_tesim: Attendees share in a toast at an International Sourdough Reunion banquet, Hotel Alexandria, Los Angeles, 1949
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 14
Text from negative sleeve: 2907. Yukon Society Convention 1949. Wm H Brown L.A.
named_subject_tesim: Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Men and women raise their glasses and share in a toast at the 18th annual International Sourdough Reunion banquet, hosted by the Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California, Hotel Alexandria, Los Angeles.

International Sourdough Reunion, Inc. is a non-profit association of fraternal organizations that share the pioneer history of Alaska and the Yukon in common. Each year, one organization hosts a convention of members from groups located as far north as Fairbanks, Alaska, and as far south as San Diego, California (with points in between). In 1949, the Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California hosted the 18th annual International Sourdough Reunion convention. Events were held at the Hotel Alexandria in Los Angeles. The four-day event (August 17-20) was noted in the article, "Gold Rush is On: Tales of '98 Relived at Sourdough Meeting," Los Angeles Times, 18 Aug. 1949: A2.
combined_subject_ssim: Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California

description_tesim: The Bartlett collection contains a copy photograph of William H. Brown and others standing outside of Brown’s cabin in the Yukon Territory in 1898. Brown was a miner in the Yukon, and hotel manager in Nome, Alaska. See image ark no. 21198/zz002d6ksv.

Attendees of a function held by the Alaska-Yukon Club of Southern California. The men and women in view wear badges - likely naming where they lived during their time as Alaska or Yukon territory pioneers. The man at center is William H. (Billy) Brown of Los Angeles. Brown served as Secretary for the Alaska Yukon Club, and as President of the International Sourdoughs.

The gathering depicted in this photo may related to a celebration depicted in a second photograph that shows William Brown and his wife in a different setting (but wearing the same attire) standing next to an anniversary cake.  See image ark no. 21198/zz002d602s.
combined_subject_ssim: Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California

title_tesim: Hollywood Knickerbocker Hotel, Ivar Street, Los Angeles, 1940s
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: C. Purnell, Santa Monica Civic Opera Association. Mr. Bartlett. [Sleeve is an envelope with “C. Purnell” as addresser, and “Mr. Bartlett” as addressee.]
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Hollywood Knickerbocker Apartments, formerly the Hollywood Knickerbocker Hotel, was built in 1925 by E.M. Frasier in the Spanish Colonial Revival Style. The hotel catered to the burgeoning film industry and was a frequent haunt of actors and musicians. The hotel became senior living apartments in the 1970s.

Photograph of the Hollywood Knickerbocker Hotel along Ivar Street, Los Angeles. Entry into the 11-story building begins at a covered walkway leading to an entry with three archways. At right sits the Terrace Restaurant; at left, the Knickerbocker Terrance Room, marked by an outdoor patio and sign advertising cocktails. The hotel is flanked by two buildings partially in view. Street lamps line both sides of Ivar Street, while 1930s era automobiles are viewed parked along the street and in the parking lot at foreground. A sign directs traffic into the lot, while an adjacent sign advertises establishments, including: […] Beauty Salon; Broadway Hollyw’d; Ivar House [restaurant]; Foreman & Clark [department store]; and [Knicker]bocker Shop. Photographed from a parking lot across the street.

description_tesim: Text from a negative sleeve linked to a similar image appears to describe this photo as well: Long Beach, fleet from roof garden. Compo..., All Year Club, Red Island RR. 1933.
This image was used in a 1933 print advertisement for the All Year Club of Southern California - a Los Angeles based travel agency that promoted low cost vacations in Southern California.

Photograph of two women seated at a table on a terrace, with a girl standing apart from them at the terrace wall. A sign for the Capitol Theatre is viewed in the immediate background at left. At center ground, the Long Beach Municipal Auditorium (demolished in 1975) with a fleet of six ships in the ocean beyond.

Probably photographed at the Breakers Hotel (210 East Ocean Avenue210 E. Ocean Boulevard), that stood adjacent to the Capitol/Tracy Theatre (demolished 1974), across from the Long Beach Municipal Auditorium (now the Long Beach Convention and Entertainment Center).

description_tesim: Date derived from a similar image of Mona Ohrtland with minaret as background (image ark no. 21198/zz002d4pbp).

Mona Ohrtland, Santa Monica Players theater group member, dressed as a Berber Ouled Naïl girl. She wears a floral print veil over her head and torso and is seated on the floor handling strewn flowers in a puddle of water with a small wooden cage and open book on either side of her. This photomontage photograph has been superimposed onto a second photograph of Middle Eastern cityscape, with palm trees and a minaret acting as a backdrop behind her. She was photographed in the home of Adelbert Bartlett (535 15th Street).

title_tesim: Demolition of an adobe building, Casa de Don Dolores Sepúlveda, Los Angeles, 1940
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2554. 12/20/40. Mrs. Beague [sic] Packman. Old adobe house. Bundy Drive WLA, 1940. See also Bartlett earlier photos …enclosed.
named_subject_tesim: Casa de Don Dolores Sepúlveda (Los Angeles, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Five men use crowbars and other tools to deconstruct a wall of adobe bricks. One young man, wearing a sombrero, stands within the adobe’s doorway frame. Remnants of spackle remain on portions of the wall, while piles of rubble litter the area at foreground. Immediately behind them rests a pile of wood planks. A second structure, with wooden frame and interior walls intact, stands in the background. The exterior walls have been removed; the wood shingle roof is in disrepair. A neighborhood with dwellings, utility poles and numerous trees can be seen in the distance.

The Casa de Don Dolores Sepúlveda was an adobe home located near San Vicente Springs at South Bundy Drive and Nebraska Ave. José Dolores Sepúlveda, b. 1826-1905, was the son of Francisco Sepúlveda, holder of the original land grant for Rancho San Vicente y Santa Monica. The home was once part of Rancho San Vicente.
combined_subject_ssim: Casa de Don Dolores Sepúlveda (Los Angeles, Calif.)

title_tesim: Clipping regarding California’s first printing press, 1956
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: C. Purnell, Santa Monica Civic Opera Association. Mr. Bartlett. [Sleeve is an envelope with “C. Purnell” as addresser, and “Mr. Bartlett” as addressee.]
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Article titled, “Story of State’s First Press Told in Old Letter,” Santa Monica Independent, 9 Aug. 1956. Clipping discusses a then 100-year-old letter written by Enos Christman, a 20-year-old printer from Pennsylvania who became a newspaper editor and printer in Sonora, California. In the letter, Christman describes the “pioneer press” that churned out “nearly all the papers then printed in California,” noting that it was the press first used by Augustin Zamorano (California’s first printer) as early as the 1830s.

title_tesim: “Merry Widow” production with Kay Marshall, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, possibly 1960
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 39
Handwritten on Gevaert film box: Merry Widow, New Moon.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in “The Merry Widow,” Act 3. A female sextet (including Kay Marshall at far left) performs alongside cast members seated at tables. The men wear tuxedos while the women seated with them are dressed as grisettes (can-can dancers). The set, with its cloth covered tables and swag drapery against the stage backdrop, is the decor of Maxim’s - a Parisian nightclub. Beyond the stage, musicians can be seen in the orchestra pit at foreground.
Possibly related to the article, “Civic Group Will Present ‘Merry Widow’,” Los Angeles Times, 12 Jun. 1960: WS5.

title_tesim: Leona Bertrand as a seer, Venice, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2821. Leona Bertrand. 7/1/37. Temple Fires Relighted. Arab costume. Portraits as seeress.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 2821
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Bertrand was a Cuban born dramatics teacher, residing in 1938 at 115 Wadsworth in Ocean Park. The city directory lists her as a dramatics teacher with Fern Severance (a drama, music and voice teacher). Severance resided (and most likely taught lessons) at 73 Market Street in Venice. Bertrand was probably photographed at this location (see images ark nos. 21198/zz002d6k62 and 21198/zz002d6k83).

Salon portrait of Leona Bertrand dressed as a seer (clairvoyant). Bertrand wears a sheer white dress with gold embellishments at the neck, chest and waist. A veil made from the same gossamer material is draped over her head and shoulders, the veil secured by an elaborate band evoking an Egyptian crown. She looks directly into the camera, hands clasped together, touching her face. She has been up lit for dramatic effect. Fabric and a sheet of glass can be viewed on the table before her. An Oriental tapestry embroidered with floral designs hangs in the background.

title_tesim: “Merry Widow” production with dancers performing, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, possibly 1960
caption_tesim: Handwritten on Gevaert film box: Merry Widow, New Moon.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 36
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Possibly related to the article, “Civic Group Will Present ‘Merry Widow’,” Los Angeles Times, 12 Jun. 1960: WS5.

Two dancers perform in a Santa Monica Civic Opera production of “The Merry Widow,” Act 2. The female performers surrounding the dancers wear the traditional clothing of a fictional Slavic country - including kokoshnik-inspired headdresses, peasant blouses, full skirts with aprons and waist cinchers. The men wear tuxedos and military dress uniforms. The setting is a garden at the home of the widow Sonia Sadoya (Hanna Glawari). Scenery flats representing trees, a faux brick wall and a lattice-work structure all contribute to the outdoor setting. Additional props include a rattan table, chairs and settee.
English language adaptations of “The Merry Widow” include several name changes. Here, names from the original Viennese work are noted in parentheses after the variation.

caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 7
Handwritten on Gevaert film box: Merry Widow, New Moon.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

description_tesim: Negative housed with envelope addressed to Adelbert Bartlett. Handwritten script reads:  “New Moon, operetta, de Parker, 1949.” (See image ark no. 21198/zz002d6p71)

Santa Monica Civic Opera singers from a production of “The New Moon.” Four men, in late 18th century costumes (long jackets, breeches, stockings, and tricorn hats), stand in front of a backdrop painted to look like a brick building with an elaborate ironwork balcony, dormered windows, decorative columns, and a pediment above the functioning doorway. The setting is the “Café Creole” in the French Quarter of New Orleans, Louisiana. A microphone can be seen at the foot of the stage.

title_tesim: "Elisir d’amore” production with Roger Hansen, John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica, 1951
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: SM Civic Opera, Pagliacci, Elixir, Cavalleria.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 57
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Opera singers performing in “L’Elisir d’amore.” The stage is filled with cast members dressed as soldiers and Basque villagers. At center, Roger Hansen  portraying Dr. Dulcamara stands on a mock-stone platform. He is in stark contrast to the villagers, wearing a top hat with peacock feather, cape and coat, waist coat (vest), striped cravat and sash, trousers and boots. He also holds a walking stick. The setting is a village square in Basque country, late 18th century, reflected in the painted backdrop depicting rolling hills and trees, and additional set pieces painted to mimic stone architecture. Tables, chairs and a bench seat complete the stage set. Opera performed at John Adams Auditorium.
“L’Elisir d’amore” was performed twice at John Adams Auditorium during the company’s 8th season: October 13 and November 3, 1951.

description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera performance of “L’Elisir d’amore.” A male singer portraying Nemorino is surrounded by adoring peasant women, most wearing full skirts and waist cinchers or lace-up bodices with peasant blouses. The setting is a village square in Basque country, late 18th century. Scenery includes a painted backdrop with rolling hills and trees, and set pieces painted to look like stone structures. Opera performed at John Adams Auditorium.
“L’Elisir d’amore” was performed twice at John Adams Auditorium during the company’s 8th season:  October 13 and November 3, 1951.

Opera performed at John Adams Auditorium.

title_tesim: Page of article describing the formation of the Salton Trough and the Salton Sea, 1952
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 1952. Salton Sea, ancient, modern.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: For additional pages, see image ark nos. 21198/zz002d7cnb, and 21198/zz002d7cmt.

Photograph of an article page (2nd page of 3) with geological illustrations and text describing changes to the Colorado Desert area and the Gulf of Mexico, leading to the creation of the Salton Sink (Salton Trough). Text below illustrations at left: “Gulf of California once filled today’s Salton Sink area. We’ve made these charts to serve as a geological guide for visitors to the Salton Sea and the whole Colorado Desert area, We based them on the most widely accepted theory of the area’s evolution: Several hundred million years ago the Gulf of California extended north as far as San Gorgonio Pass. The Colorado River dumped into the gulf about where Yuma is today, gradually building a huge delta westward from its mouth.” [Text at right:] “In later prehistoric age, silt dam cut off gulf from Salton Sink area. As the silt piled up, it finally formed an earth dam which cut the Gulf of California in two and left an orphan salt water lake in the north. With no water to replace that lost by evaporation, the lake dried up-leaving an arid, 1,000-foot-deep basin which has been named the Salton Sink. (From Yuma to its mouth, the Colorado falls about 140 feet; the drop from Yuma to the Salton Sea is nearly 400 feet. Thus the next series of changes.)” Publication unidentified. Article dated February 1952, reprint date unknown.

description_tesim: The parade route began on 4th St. at Colorado Ave., followed north along 4th St. to Wilshire Blvd., and turned westward toward Ocean Ave. where it moved south. The procession passed the review stand at Ocean and Broadway before ending at Pico Blvd.

This photograph shows Mayor Gillette  along the second half of the parade route, traveling south on Ocean Avenue. A second photo shows him traveling north on 4th St. near Santa Monica Blvd. (image ark no. 21198/zz002d7fqb).

Photograph of Mayor Edmond Gillette riding on horseback alongside military men (possibly Navy) in the California Admission Day Parade. Gillette is viewed at left at left wearing western attire (cowboy hat, boots and a gun belt). The men walking alongside him wear military visor caps with insignias, and pullover jackets/shirts with collars. They follow the second half of the parade route south along Ocean Avenue. Spectators are viewed in the background, seated and standing in front of two buildings. The one on the left is the Georgian Hotel.

In 1937, Santa Monica hosted the California Admission Day celebration and convention of the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West. The three-day event (held September 8-10), commemorated the 87th anniversary of California’s admission to the Union. The Los Angeles Times published several articles between September 7th and 10th that described planned festivities, but a more detailed account of the celebration can be found in The Grizzly Bear, a magazine published by the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West (1905-1954). The article is titled, "Happy Memories of Admission Day," The Grizzly Bear, 1 Oct. 1937: 3.
combined_subject_ssim: Native Daughters of the Golden West
Native Sons of the Golden West

title_tesim: Page of article describing the formation of the Salton Trough and the Salton Sea, 1952
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 1952. Salton Sea, ancient, modern.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of an article page (3rd page of 3) with geological illustrations and text describing events leading to the transformation of the Salton Sink (Salton Trough) into the Salton Sea. Text below illustrations at left: “Five centuries ago, Colorado River formed Lake Cahuilla. A few million years later, the Colorado abandoned its mouth at the gulf, took a course to the west, and began filling the Salton Sink. Later the Colorado shifted back to the gulf and the lake dried up. Since then, the sink has been alternately lake and desert. Most recently, from about 900 A.D. to 1400 A.D., it was filled by Lake Cahuilla, whose beach scars are visible around the basin today.”[Text at right:]  “In 1905, Colorado broke loose, formed today’s Salton Sea. When first discovered by Spanish explorer Melchior Diaz in 1540, the sink was desert-and so it remained until floods in 1905 sent the Colorado rampaging through the Imperial Canal and into the sink to form today’s Salton Sea. After a dramatic two-year fight, the levees were repaired and the river was turned to its course, Today, Imperial, Parker, and the other dams that hold the river in check maintain a status quo.” Publication unidentified. Article dated February 1952, reprint date unknown.
For additional pages, see image ark nos. 21198/zz002d7cnb, and 21198/zz002d7ck9.

description_tesim: The parade route began on 4th St. at Colorado Ave., followed north along 4th St. to Wilshire Blvd., and turned westward toward Ocean Ave. where it moved south. The procession passed the review stand at Ocean and Broadway before ending at Pico Blvd.

Three men, seated on horseback, wave to the camera during the California Admission Day Parade. The men wear caballero attire - wide-brimmed hats, short jackets and pants (both embroidered). Two other men, wearing western attire, are viewed in the background at right. They were photographed along the second half of the parade route on Ocean Avenue. A small portion of the Georgian Hotel can be seen in the background at right.

In 1937, Santa Monica hosted the California Admission Day celebration and convention of the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West. The three-day event (held September 8-10), commemorated the 87th anniversary of California’s admission to the Union. The Los Angeles Times published several articles between September 7th and 10th that described planned festivities, but a more detailed account of the celebration can be found in The Grizzly Bear, a magazine published by the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West (1905-1954). The article is titled, "Happy Memories of Admission Day," The Grizzly Bear, 1 Oct. 1937: 3.
combined_subject_ssim: Native Daughters of the Golden West
Native Sons of the Golden West

title_tesim: Caballeros on horseback in the California Admission Day Parade, Santa Monica, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2847. Admission Day 1937. Santa Monica Parade. Bill Hart Jr, Irvin S Cobb, Leo Carrillo.
named_subject_tesim: Native Daughters of the Golden West
Native Sons of the Golden West
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The parade route began on 4th St. at Colorado Ave., followed north along 4th St. to Wilshire Blvd., and turned westward toward Ocean Ave. where it moved south. The procession passed the review stand at Ocean and Broadway before ending at Pico Blvd.

Photograph of three men, wearing caballero attire, seated on horseback, riding in the California Admission Day Parade. The men wear wide-brimmed hats, short jackets and pants (both embroidered). Three other men, wearing western attire, are viewed in the background. They were photographed along the second half of the parade route on Ocean Avenue. A portion of the Georgian Hotel can be seen in the background at right.

In 1937, Santa Monica hosted the California Admission Day celebration and convention of the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West. The three-day event (held September 8-10), commemorated the 87th anniversary of California’s admission to the Union. The Los Angeles Times published several articles between September 7th and 10th that described planned festivities, but a more detailed account of the celebration can be found in The Grizzly Bear, a magazine published by the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West (1905-1954). The article is titled, "Happy Memories of Admission Day," The Grizzly Bear, 1 Oct. 1937: 3.
combined_subject_ssim: Native Daughters of the Golden West
Native Sons of the Golden West

description_tesim: The Children's Home Society of California is a non-profit child welfare agency founded in 1891. It became the first licensed child placement agency in California in 1911. description_tesim: At some point after WWII the rustic branch fence at Palisades park was replaced with two types: a pre-cast concrete fence with a criss-cross design and a wood board fence. Bartlett photographs of Palisades Park dated 1946 show the rustic branch fence and the wood rail fence.

View facing southeast towards Santa Monica Pier from Palisades Park. People enjoy the park, including a young woman standing among palm trees a couple seated on a bench and a couple at the cliff-edge rustic eucalyptus branch fence.

title_tesim: Street scene at Cajon St. toward East Citrus Ave., Redlands, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2852. Redlands, San Bernardino, 1937.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 2852
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Fisher Building (built 1899, demolished 1964, currently Redlands City Hall) was named after Henry Fisher, a Redlands pioneer responsible, in part, for developing the areas resources. Fisher invested in business properties and helped finance the Redlands Electric Light & Power Company, and the Southern California Power Company. These two entities eventually merged and became Southern California Edison Electric. Fisher also organized the Redlands Central Railway Company and the Redlands Street Railway Company amongst other accomplishments.

Photograph of Cajon Street toward E. Citrus Avenue (where Cajon becomes N. Orange Street). At foreground right stands the Fisher Building. A vertical sign reading "Edison" hangs from the building's cupola (seen at left). Arched windows are trimmed with decorative molding and shaded with striped awnings. Additional businesses on the Downtown Redlands street include a dentist, drug stores, and a café. A sign in the extreme distance reads: "Hotel Casa Loma."

description_tesim: Pioneer Day is an official holiday celebrated in the U.S. state of Utah, with some celebrations in regions of surrounding states originally settled by Mormon pioneers. In 1938, Pioneer Day was celebrated on July 23 in Ocean Park, a neighborhood of Santa Monica. It was organized under the auspices of the society, California Daughters of Pioneers, and the presidents of five Southland Mormon stakes, and directed by General Chairman O. H. Hewlett. The day was spent in speeches, picnicking and reunion
Related to Los Angeles Times article, “Utah Rally Will Be Tomorrow: Twelve Thousand to Attend Reunion at Ocean Park," 22 Jul. 1938: 14.
Photograph of 7 women in a park dressed as mid-19th century pioneers at the annual Pioneer Day event. They are probably members of the California Daughters of Pioneers.

description_tesim: Photograph of businesses and street rail lines along North 'E' Street. Rail line cables are viewed overhead, following the tracks below. Signage on buildings and awnings read: "Serving Luncheon & Dinner Mapes Cafeteria, Court and 'D' Sts, - 1 1/2 Blocks East - 1/2 Block North," "Hotel," "Sally's," "Central Hotel," "Stoners Shoes," Gallen Kamps All Leather $3.95 Shoes." (Gallen Kamps is under construction in this photo). Three additional businesses can be viewed on the left leading up to the Bank of America (housed in the Andreson Building, 320 N. 'E' Street). Additional establishments include a dentist's office, a drug store, and a business selling "giant malts."

description_tesim: Banning House, or the General Phineas Banning Residence Museum, is located in the Wilmington neighborhood of Los Angeles. The home (built in 1863) includes a garden with some of the earliest eucalyptus trees planted in California, along with wisteria brought from Japan and planted on the estate in the late 1890s.

Photograph of two women wearing tiaras, satin gowns,  and capes with fur-trimmed hoods. They hold a bundle of wisteria stems between them and are flanked by two drum majorettes in uniform. Festival participants stand in the back ground, most looking toward the camera. They are photographed below a flowering vine of wisteria.

In 1938, Pioneer Day was celebrated on July 23 in Ocean Park, a neighborhood of Santa Monica. It was organized under the auspices of the society, California Daughters of Pioneers, and the presidents of five Southland Mormon stakes, and directed by General Chairman O. H. Hewlett. title_tesim: Owner Charles B. Hervey and a woman in the patio of El Mirasol Hotel, Santa Barbara, 1941
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: El Mirasol Hotel, Santa Barbara. 7/10/41, C. B.  Hervey. [Crossed out:] J. B. Quigley. Miss Katherine...Rodenwald.

description_tesim: El Mirasol began as a mansion built by Mary Herter, the mother of the artist Albert Herter. After his mother's death, Herter renovated the mansion and converted it into a boutique hotel. The hotel was purchased by Charles B. Hervey from John B. Quigley in 1941. The city block site of the former El Mirasol Hotel was dedicated as Alice Keck Park in 1980. The block is bounded by Santa Barbara, Micheltorena, Garden, and Arrellaga Streets.

View into the patio of the El Mirasol Hotel with a fountain in the center, ornamented with potted plants and 2 statues of cranes. A woman and Charles B. Hervey are seated in wicker chairs placed beneath an arch of the patio arcade. A vine trails from the arcade roof.

description_tesim: Night time view of the Peacemakers mural over the Western Gateway in the Court of Pacifica, flanked by the names of the world's boldest explorers. Peacemakers was created by the sisters Helen, Esther and Margaret Bruton, artists active in northern California. The mural combines figures in bas-relief with painted areas. At the base of the wall (R) is a poster advertising the "Eddy Duchin Orchestra."
The mural was created by the sisters Margaret, Helen and Esther Bruton. It was 144 feet long and 57 feet high.

title_tesim: Man diving into El Mirador hotel pool, Palm Springs, 1941
caption_tesim: Text on negative sleeve: No. 2761-P410-421, El Mirador Hotel, Palm Springs, Pool - 2/1/41
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This is a picture taken at the historic El Mirador Hotel in Palm Springs, regarded one of the finest luxury hotels of its time. This is a picture of its famous Olympic-size swimming pool that had five diving boards, an underwater window on the pool for use by photographers, and a pool observation platform. We can see a swimmer diving off one of the boards, as guests watch along the observation deck. In the background, we can see the most defining feature of the hotel, a pink Byzantine tower from which the hotel took its name — El Mirador — meaning "The Lookout."

description_tesim: Photographs housed with this negative sleeve are related to others from a sleeve labeled "Carolyn's negatives." The photographs are visually and thematically similar. In addition, both sleeves contained photographs of Santa Monica High School structures built between 1937 and 1939 by the Works Project Administration (WPA). Both sleeves also included construction site images, likely related to the WPA’s reconstruction project (image ark nos. 21198/zz002d8j8h and 198/zz002d8k9h).

Photograph of a still life set-up, perhaps for art class instruction, comprised of a Bodhisattva figurine set in the foreground, a small vessel holding flowers is placed slightly behind. A scroll-like textile is draped over a two-paneled divider. The composition is set on a table in front of a window. Photographed in a classroom at Santa Monica High School, probably by Carolyn Bartlett.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica High School (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: Drum majors and majorettes. at the Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade, Santa Monica, 1936
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2927, Ocean Park, Children's Parade, 1936
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The event was covered in a Los Angeles Times article, "Hundreds of Children to be in Floral Parade," 27 Aug. 1936: 10.

The Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade was a community event started in 1919. The 1936 parade was organized by Santa Monica club women and civic leaders, with Mayor Gillette and the city council also lending their support. Flowers were provided by city parks and private gardens.

Photograph of crowds at the Seventeenth Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade. At foreground are drum majors and majorettes. They each hold batons and wear their respective uniforms. Most wear shako hats topped with pompoms or feather plumes. One child standing at foreground wears an "Uncle Sam" hat. In the background, crowds of adults sit and stand in front of buildings, watching a procession of accordion players parade in front of them. Signs on the buildings and windows read: "St. Regis Hotel," "Hotel Dorr," "Hotel Edmund and Apartments", "Dairy & Vegetarian Cafeteria, N. Ginsgerg mgr," "Waserman" [groceries and meat], "Fleishcher's Restaurant," and "Casa d'Amore Italian Restaurant & Pizzeria."

The Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade was a community event started in 1919. The 1936 parade was organized by Santa Monica club women and civic leaders, with Mayor Gillette and the city council also lending their support. Flowers were provided by city parks and private gardens.

Photograph of a girl representing California at the Seventeenth Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade. She wears a sunray headdress and a dress with a star-covered bodice and hoopskirt decorated with oranges. She holds a banner with "California" printed at center. A cluster of oranges hang from the left side; posies hang from the right. A stuffed bear is fixed at the top. The girl is flanked by two majorettes hold batons and wear shako hats topped with pompoms, satin shirts, and straight-leg pants with a stripe along the side.

title_tesim: Photograph of newspaper article, Pretty Chinese Star Visitor Here, Tells Her "Only" 2 Wishes, circa 1929-1933
caption_tesim: Text reads: Pretty Chinese Star Visitor Here, Tells Her "Only" 2 Wishes.  Miss Olive Young, Small and Smiling, Is Versatile Actress; Speaks English Fluently and Is Rehearsing for Stage Show.  By Paul Walton.  Mary Pickford has her following and fans in this country, and Miss Olive Young, small and smiling Chinese actress, who is now visiting in the home of Dr. T.B. Chew, 610 Alta avenue, has her fan mail daily from the many admirers of her talents among Chinese audiences throughout the world.  Yesterday afternoon the pretty Chinese screen idol consented to an interview in the home of Dr. Chew.  She wore an Oriental wrap, and in almost too-perfect English, she chatted pleasantly about her career, pointing occasionally to press clippings in a ponderous scrap book.  Born In U.S.  Miss Young, though born in the United States, spent much of her time in the far east.  She is a contemporary of Mei Lang Fang, and though the latter has never appeared in moving pictures, they are the Pickford-Fairbanks of China.  While in China she accompanied one of her British productions on a personal apperance tour that took her into the important cities in the east.  During her trip, she played more than six months in Java, Malaya, Burma, and throughout the Dutch West Indies.  Speaking of Chinese productions, she said, "While American pictures were more popular in the colonial settlements, the British-made Chinese screen versions of many of the old historical and dramatical incidents of old Cathay, appeal more to the tastes of the purely Chinese audiences."  She was featured in many of these pictures.  Is Versatile.  Such productions were made as silents, and contained both English and Chinese subtitles.  According to her friends, the audience lost something because of that, as she can speak either Chinese or English with equal ease and fluency.  Able to play a part in either a drama built around some incident of history or tradition of an old dynasty, or to portray the modern version of an oriental character, Miss Young, when talking of her future, wished for only two things.  Has Two Wishes.  First of all, she wished to be able to organize a strickly Chinese moving picture company in America, that with the aid of American experience and equipment, could make some of the colorful incidents of China occur again in films of today.  Secondly, she wants to portray parts in these productions where she can express the feelings of the new world and old China.  Miss Young is at present rehearsing for a stage production in Los Angeles that will be presented to the Chinese colony there soon.
Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2382, 12/1/29, Tennyson, Walter, 7014 Fountain Ave, Statues, Hollywood, Tony Burke

description_tesim: The Hotel Playa Ensenada opened on October 31, 1930. The hotel was closed and partly demolished in 1964. Partly rebuilt in 1978, the hotel was reopened as the Centro Social, Cívico y Cultural de Ensenada

title_tesim: Woman with snowman, Mount Baldy, 1928
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2378.  Camp Baldy.  1928.  Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif.  A mile in elevation above some of Southern California's flower gardens and orange groves are snowfields where in negatives enclosed co-eds on a late November week-end built snow men--"just had to get a man somehow"--and modelled a very expressive "snow bear" in honor of the California bear, official state "animule."  One view shows Grace Healy offering the bear a cup of snow.

title_tesim: Typewritten documents describing photographs of shipments of Easter lilies from Los Angeles, 1931
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: Bartlett 1048 - 1049 - 1050 1051 - 1052 - 1053 Easter Lillies. Easter Lillies, Los Angeles nursery, where 500,000, a million dollars worth of these blooms are coming into full flowering for the Easter trade. Girls, left to right, loading flowers into truck - Rosine McDougall, Grace Wilkiemeyer, Marjorie Franklin. Closeup, one girl, with flowers, Rosine McDougall. Closeup, one girl, with Bob Lloyd (aviator), Grace Wilkiemeyer.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
ark:/21198/zz002bdj6f (negative)
description_tesim: Not all images described in document are in digital collection

5 short typewritten documents, probably adhesive label captions for photographs, with edges torn.  Text reads in part: 1. 1931 514 BA Delivering Easter flowers by air.  Bob Lloyd receiving a box of California Easter lillues [sic] for shipment ot [sic] the Jane Addams settlement in Chicago, the gift of high school girls in Glendale, Cal.  2. California's half million dollar Easter lilly crop.  Scene in the hothouse where easter lillies are in full bloom.  3. 1931 512 BA Shipping Easter Lillies by air.  Pilot Bob Lloyd, aerial flower delivery agent, receives boxes of Easter lillies.  4. 1931 513 BA California's half million dollar Easter lilly crop is ready for shipment.  5. Loading part of California's half million dollar easter lillies into trucks for shipment to the markets throughout the country.  Left to right Rosine McDougall, Grace Wilkiemeyer, and Marjorie Franklin.

title_tesim: Japanese style flower arrangement with roses by Margaret Preininger, Los Angeles, 1935
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Miss Preininger- Flowers- Los Angeles Junior College. 1935
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Roses in a cylindrical, glass vase in a small table

Margaret Preininger published a book titled Japanese flower arrangement for modern homes on December 1, 1935. At that time she was the chairman of Cultural Arts at Los Angeles Junior College. According to the review in Kirkus Reviews, the 35 pages of text explain the four important Japanese Schools and the adaptations of their principles to American flowers and settings; the book includes 52 large reproductions of arrangements and 100 line drawings showing method of procedure; and chapters on vases, holders, flower selection, color, seasons, background and table settings

title_tesim: Children playing pin the tail on the bunny, Santa Monica, 1931
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 1056, Easter Bunny.  1931, 518, BA.  AN EASTER BUNNY GAME FOR CHILDREN.  Santa Monica, Cal...Here's a new pastime for children, especially at Easter time.  Instead of pinning the tail on a donkey, a bunny is represented on the canvas and the progress of the game is the same.  Here Dorothy Stone holds the bunny while Gwennie Stone tries her luck.

title_tesim: Typewritten description of photographs of Neal Dodd and St. Mary of the Angels Church, 1931
caption_tesim: Text reads, in part: 7/15/31, 2542, Adelbert Bartlett, Santa Monica.  Father Neal Dodd, world famous "Padre of the Movies," pastor of St. Mary of the Angels (Episcopal), celebrates his twelfth year as a Hollywood pastor during which he not only has attended to the spiritual needs of a growing flock which includes a number of motion picture folk but has performed fully 95 per cent. of the weddings filmed and recorded in Hollywood studios during this time. ... has served as technical advisor ... has wed practically every motion picture player of prominence for art's sake.  But Father Dodd has not neglected regular masses and sermons and real weddings and other duties of his parish ... his choir boys ... besides appearing at the church of St. Mary of the Angels ... are present singing in the choir lofts on motion picture sets whenever their pastor officiates at film weddings.
Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2542, Neal Dodd, Church of St Mary of the Angels Hollywood

ark:/21198/zz002bx8dd (print)
ark:/21198/zz002bx8cw (print)
ark:/21198/zz002bx8gf (print)
description_tesim: Typewritten text reads, in part: The spirit of French aviation, a tableau posed recently [handwritten: 1928] for by the attractive Misses Patsy Mamar ... and Shirley Widman ... in the cockpit of a biplane ... formerly owned in France by Captain Charles Nungesser ... the flying machine was on public exhibition at Clover Field, Santa Monica ... when a vandal mutilated the fuselage by cutting out the famous Nungesser emblem ... Jim Granger ... now owns the plane.  Handwritten at top right: 1928

Text of newspaper article reads, in part: Vandals Mutilate World War Relic.  Nungesser Insignia Cut From Fuselage of Famous Plane.  One of the priceless relics of the World war, a fighting plane flown by Captain Charles Nungesser, the French ace, has been mutilated by one of the grossest instances of vandalism to come to the attention of police ... Working with a sharp knife, the vandal cut from the side of the fuselage the famous Nungesser emblem ... Removal of the entire panel was not noted until a few days ago and makes the plane almost worthless.  Handwritten on clipping: Santa Monica, California, 3/16/31

Charles Nungesser was a successful French WWI fighter pilot, performed in air shows and movies in the United States after the war, and disappeared, with his navigator Francois Coli, during an attempted trans-Atlantic flight in 1927.

Nungesser's Hanriot HD.1 was owned by Jim Granger or his widow from 1927 to 1951 (vandalized 1931 at Clover Field, Santa Monica; put in storage 1933), when it was purchased by Edward T. Maloney, restored, and put on display at the Planes of Fame Air Museum in Chino, California.

title_tesim: Aerial coastline view of the Rancho Malibu la Costa development area, Malibu, circa 1927
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: View from hill, possibly from the end of Rambla Vista, towards cars on the Pacific Coast Highway and coast in the area of the Rancho Malibu la Costa development.

Rancho Topanga Malibu Sequit was a 13,316-acre Spanish land grant given by Spanish Governor José Joaquín de Arrillaga in 1804 to José Bartolomé Tapia. In 1892 Frederick Hastings Rindge and May Knight Rindge, purchased the rancho. In 1926, the Marblehead Land Company, formed by May Knight Rindge, made a $6 million deal with Harold G. Ferguson to develop the La Costa area (between Las Flores Canyon and Carbon Canyon), but that deal fell apart when Ferguson was sentenced to jail for securities fraud in 1931. Ferguson laid out the development with street names as they remain today: Rambla Vista, Paseo Serra, etc. It took until the mid 1930's for development of the area to begin in earnest.

title_tesim: Typewritten account of the celebration of the creation 1,250,000th cone at Jacks Famous Ice Cream Cones (p. 1), Venice, 1928
caption_tesim: The text reads, in part: Negatives show Jack Halstein, world's champion ice cream cone baker and his ornate tiled concession on the Abbot Kinney Million Dollar Pier at Venice-of-America, California on the Pacific (Los Angeles beach). On May 1 Jack Halstein, veteran concessionaire at the famous Venice (Los Angeles beach) amusement, the Coney Island of the West, expects to celebrate his personal baking of the one and one quarter million crispy cones after his own recipe, which filled with “double-decks” of luscious ice creams of many flavors especially frozen by ice cream factories for Jack have tickled the palates of young and old on the West Coast for the past sixteen years or since Jack entered “big time” as an ice cream cone vendor. But the 1,250,000 cones Jack personally has baked is by no means all of the cones he has sold, for in rush seasons the demand and is so great that he has to mix up batter by the 60-gallon barrel and employ a number of sweet girl assistants to bake the cones on a revolving griddle; in fact, Jack’s sales have totaled some 5,000,000 cones to date. Jack estimates that his mouth-watering cones, placed end to end, would stretch from Labrador to Patagonia, with a few carloads dropped off in New York, Chicago and Walla Walla, Wash., for good measure. He further declares that if all the “Oh my’s they’re good!” [“this is good” is crossed out and replaced with “they’re good!”] were put together they would produce a chorus louder than all the musical comedy choruses put together on Broadway and would make [“produce” is crossed out and replaced with “make”] so much melody they would put Rudy Valee and the radios out of business.

title_tesim: Typewritten account of the celebration of the creation 1,250,000th cone at Jacks Famous Ice Cream Cones (p. 2), Venice, 1928
caption_tesim: The text reads: Jack Halstein’s friendly competitors in the ice cream cone business are planning a special tribute to him on May 1st, when he will announce the grand total of 1,250,000 personally baked cones. These figures will be official, as coming from the Abbot Kinney Co., owners of the Venice amusement pier, to which Jack regularly pays rent for his elaborate and expensive cone stand right in the middle of the broad thorofare which extents out over the waves along the massive pier which is lined with the finest and newest of amusement devices. Friends of Jack are breathlessly awaiting the event, which is to be kept a deep dark secret, until the day.

title_tesim: Cave entrance with rock carving of Abraham Lincoln, Los Angeles, 1930
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2464. P 1-2-3-4-5-6. Cave. Sawtelle. Cave. Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif. Mysterious cave, with plaque of Lincoln carved above entrance, and figures of Egyptians within, discovered recently by Boy Scouts in Santa Monica mountains near Sawtelle, California. Officials at National Military Home there state the cave was work of colored veteran some 25 years ago. The man would disappear for days in the hills. He was known to have been a devotee of voodoo magic. (Clarice Barclay stands outside door.)

Built in 1924 the villa Dar Marroc was modeled on Moroccan residences inTangiers by the Scottish artist, Gordon Coutts (1865-1937). The villa and surrounding property were owned by the silent screen actor and neighbor J. Carol Naish. The villa became part of the  Korakia Pensione in 1989.

Margaret Preininger published a book titled Japanese flower arrangement for modern homes on December 1, 1935. At that time she was the chairman of Cultural Arts at Los Angeles Junior College. According to the review in Kirkus Reviews, the 35 pages of text explain the four important Japanese Schools and the adaptations of their principles to American flowers and settings; the book includes 52 large reproductions of arrangements and 100 line drawings showing method of procedure; and chapters on vases, holders, flower selection, color, seasons, background and table settings

Eugene R. Plummer and his family once owned sections of Los Angeles including the Hancock Park area, mid-Wilshire, Hollywood Hills, and West Hollywood. The site of his home was purchased by the County of Los Angeles in 1937 and is known as Plummer Park, West Hollywood.
8th annual Pioneers of Hollywood picnic and reunion, Plummer Park, Hollywood. Cf. Los Angeles Times, August 2, 1931
A similar photograph appears with the Los Angeles Times article Hollywood Pioneers Gather, Early Day Music Featured at Reunion, August 2, 1931; dancer is identified as Verne Montesa

title_tesim: Typewritten press release describing discovery of cave and carvings, Los Angeles, 1930
caption_tesim: Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif. Exclusive news bureau. First photos ever taken of this storyA party of Boy Scouts […] recent hike through the canyons of the Santa Monica mountains ba[…] […]f the National Military Home, Pacific Branch, at Sawtelle, nea[…] […]s Angeles, Calif., came upon a mysterious abandoned cave dwell[…] which had been carved from a red sandstone cliff hidden by an oa[…] […]e and overhanging vines. Above the opening of th[…] […]ve the Scouts were surprised to note an excellent likeness of A[braha]m Lincoln, while inside the cave, which measures some 10 feet sq[…] and 8 feet high, at the back wall was decorated with a squat figure of an Egyptian god […]th right hand upraised and behind him the figure of a smaller Ori[…] with spear, all carved from the sandstone backwall of the cave[…].f. Stone shelves and a divan below a grinning mask were also […]ed. Under the smole-blackened skirt of the Egyptian god is a “fireplace,” the smoke fr[…] […]ich passed outside through a hole bored through the cave’s wall. The date “1905” is carved on the Egyptian god’s right side. Rat[…] long made their nest in the “fireplace”. Inquiry at the National […]ary Home a few miles below brought forth the conjecture that unusual dwelling place and the carvings must have been the work […] colored veteran, a former stone mason, who, as a member of te Home 25 years ago used to disappear for days in the hills. The colored soldier was to have been a devotee of voodoo worship, and it is supposed that […]d his cronies used to gather here for the practice of their myseri[…] […]ites while owls hooted and coyotes, Western wolves, bayed to the moon[…] […]idges and in shadowy canyons in the vicinity. ###

description_tesim: The Hotel Playa Ensenada opened on October 31, 1930. Later known as the Hotel Riviera del Pacifico, it was an elegant gambling casino developed by the North American Compañía Mexicana del Rosarito. The architect was Gordon E. Mayer. The hotel was closed and partly demolished in 1964. Partly rebuilt in 1978, the hotel was reopened as the Centro Social, Cívico y Cultural de Ensenada

Tree house of Amos Aspey, civil war veteran, near National Military Home, Pacific branch, Sawtelle. Cal.  House contains, parlor, bedroom, kitchen, roof garden, and is piped with water and gas. Stairs lead up to house.  House is built in a eucalyptus tree

title_tesim: Souvenir stand with hanging sarapes, Tijuana, 1931
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: Tia Juana.  Bartlett.  Outside the "longest bar in the world" at Tia Juana, which is thronged with Eastern tenderfeet during the summer overland vacation season, hovers the native vendor of tourist goods, and he speaks good "Engleese;" will if you desire, escort you through various back doors of the little town, especially when he sees two husky Mexican policemen, each wearing a brace of guns, extra ammunition, handcuffs, blackjacks and other implements of war, in their motor truck, which is a travell-jail, leave for manhunt or something in the outskirts of town.  Miss Jean Myras, Hollywood model, trying her best not to look "professional" in picture.  1931

title_tesim: Typewritten captions for photographs of Tijuana, 1931
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: Tia Juana.  Bartlett.  Outside the "longest bar in the world" at Tia Juana, which is thronged with Eastern tenderfeet during the summer overland vacation season, hovers the native vendor of tourist goods, and he speaks good "Engleese;" will if you desire, escort you through various back doors of the little town, especially when he sees two husky Mexican policemen, each wearing a brace of guns, extra ammunition, handcuffs, blackjacks and other implements of war, in their motor truck, which is a travell-jail, leave for manhunt or something in the outskirts of town.  Miss Jean Myras, Hollywood model, trying her best not to look "professional" in picture.  1931

Text reads: Bartlett.  Street scenes in Tia Juana.  Typical small rural Mexican carreta or cart and donkey. The donkey stands all day, while an occasional tourist poses with him for the master's tintype camera.  Sleepy daytime view of exterior of what is at night a roaring tobacco-smoke and liquor-fume filled dive which is almost too much for your timid tourist from Eastern states

title_tesim: Typewritten narrative about Los Angeles Library murals and Dean Cornwell (pg. 2), 1932
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2334 p 1 to 14, 1932, Los Angeles Library, Dean Cornwell, Artist, 206 E. 33rd St NY city, Los Angeles Library.

[...] Caption. Dean Cornwell. the illustrative work given him by Ray Long, Editor of the Cosmopolitan Magazine who gave him a free hand in origination of the famous vignette which ran across two pages. At the height of his popularity as an illustrator, Cornwell left New York to study and work with Frank Brangwyn, conceded to be the greatest modern mural painter. For two years he worked with Brangqyn absorbing his ideas and technique, and executing Brangwyn contracts. Returning to New York, he was offered the Hearst contract for illustrative work, which did not blind him to the greater lure of career of mural painter. Exhibits of his paintings brought much recognition, commissions for art panels, and membership in the National Galleries Association and the Architect's League. Competition for the Los Angeles contract found him an eager aspirant, and his virile, colorful sketches together with his enthusiastic grasp of the spirit of the Goodhue building, won him the commendation of the Los Angeles Library and Art Commission. The Library murals will consist of four huge panels 1 9 ft. b 19 ft. x 38 ft. with vignettes above, and eight smaller panels 19 ft. x 12 ft. The large panels will depict historical events of California, interwoven in a Gothic-like tapestry in semi-realistic allegorical fashion. The painting will probably be done in Cornwell's London studio. The murals will be painted on seamless linen canvasses woven in Belgium on special looms. The Library Commission is an enviable one because of the prominence of this Pacific Coast Goodhue Building, and because of the unusal [sic] size of the panels.

description_tesim: This narrative is about Dean Cornwell, an American painter and illustrator who was prominent in the early 20th century. His works include illustrations for Harper's Bazaar, Cosmopolitan, and Good Housekeeping. He also painted several other murals besides those for the L.A. library, including for the locations: the Warwick Hotel New York, 10 Rockefeller Plaza New York, and the Sevier State Office Building in Tennessee. He is mentioned in several L.A. Times articles, including one dated June 21, 1931, which covers his research process for the murals for the L.A. library.

description_tesim: Dean Cornwell was an American painter and illustrator who was prominent in the early 20th century. His works include illustrations for Harper's Bazaar, Cosmopolitan, and Good Housekeeping. He also painted several other murals besides those for the Los Angeles Public (Central) Library, including for the locations: the Warwick Hotel New York, 10 Rockefeller Plaza New York, and the Sevier State Office Building in Tennessee. He is mentioned in several L.A. Times articles, including one dated June 21, 1931, which covers his research process for the murals for the Los Angeles Public (Central) Library.

Portrait of Dean Cornwell standing in of the front of the Los Angeles Public (Central) Library. At the time he was working on his mural, the "Four Great Eras of California History," located on the walls beneath the rotunda. Cornwell is wearing a suit and tie, holding a briefcase in one hand. In his other hand is a lit cigarette. His hat rests on the low wall beside him, and he has one foot raised, resting on a ledge.
combined_subject_ssim: Los Angeles Public Library. Central Library

caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2761 P46
Text from negative sleeve: 2761 P43 to 46. Palm Springs. The Oasis Hotel.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Oasis Hotel, designed by Lloyd Wright (eldest son of Frank Lloyd Wright), constructed 1923-25, located at what is today the southwest corner of Palm Canyon Drive and Tahquitz Canyon Way.

View of the hotel, courtyard, and trellis covered walkway. The walkway has a concrete path and columns with potted plants at the base of each in view, and a trellis roof covered in vines and twigs. A manicured lawn, trees and bushes populate the space around it. Three guests are photographed at mid-ground – one standing and carrying a briefcase. The woman in the covered lawn chair appears to be wearing a cloche hat. The hotel stands in the background. Photographed at the Oasis Hotel, Palm Springs.

description_tesim: This image is of a crowd of onlookers viewing a recovered Japanese submarine. Several people stand up on a platform next to the submarine and look in its portholes. Other people mill around in front of the submarine, including a soldier in uniform. In the far background is a sign on a building that reads “Altman’s Fine Furniture Moderately Priced 1344 Fourth Street”.
This image is likely of the captured Japanese submarine mentioned in the L.A. Times article from Dec. 17, 1941.

title_tesim: Newspaper print of poem and note by Ina Donna Coolbrith, 1926, rephotographed, 1953
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: "2954 File B Ina Coolbrith Poetry Laureate California 1915-1928 John Crum Collection Miss Dane Gorrell of Marin County model with plaque year 1953".
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Right side of page is a hand written note by Ina Donna Cooolbrith, which reads: "I would wish that California might bring forth more of its old ideals and good penmanship; be more of the indimensional [sic] California than it now is, it is too Big to ...to and...- Ina Coolbrith".

Left side of page is poem by Ina Coolbrith, "A New Leaf", which reads: "Here's a volume, stain nor blot Mars a leaf today. Sin and folly they are not, Sorrow is away. Look, each page is white and clear And 'tis morning of the year. Of the days that swiftly run, This will not be mute. Good or evil, said and done, Sweet or bitter fruit. What shall be the record here, At the evening of the year? INA COOLBRITH".
Image is photograph of excerpt of Oakland the Post Enquirer article, dated January 2, 1926.

title_tesim: Poet Ina Donna Coolbrith article with 1880's portrait and 1888 shell wreath, Santa Monica, 1953
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2954 File B Ina Coolbrith Poetry Laureate California 1915-1928 John Crum Collection Miss Dane Gorrell of Marin County model with plaque year 1953
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Ornate 1888 horse-shoe-shaped shell art wreath made by poet Ina Donna Coolbrith  in upper left corner.  In upper right corner is a portrait of Ina Donna Coolbrith from the frontispiece of her book Songs from the Golden Gate, 1895.  The lower portion of the page is an article discussing In Coolbrith making the wreath ["Heirloom Shows Poetess' Art," Los Angeles Times, October 12, 1953]. At the time of this photograph the wreath belonged to the Perrys' nephew John H. Crum of Santa Monica. The shell wreath is an example of Victorian era domestic crafts.

title_tesim: Windemere Hotel lobby and entrance to Bamboo Room lounge, Santa Monica, 1955
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: John Lindsay, architect, Sepulveda Blvd, 1955, Lady Windermere Hotel, Before Dean Train decorating.

description_tesim: The Windemere lobby and entrance to a lounge are visible. The sign next to the door of the lounge reads "THE BAMBOO ROOM OPEN 4:00PM MON THRU FRI. 12 NOON SAT.-SUN. ENTERTAINMENT DINING-DACING NIGHTLY". A second sign on an easel to the left reads "BAMBOO ROOM PRESENTS DAVE SLOAN ORCH 5.45 PM TIL 2.00AM DANCING EVERY NITE EXCEPT MONDAY OPEN WEEK DAYS 4 PM WEEK ENDS 12 NOON".
John Lindsay, a southern California architect, was involved in the renovation of the Windemere Hotel in 1955.

description_tesim: Image is photograph of an article in Oakland The Post Enquirer, "Ina Coolbrith Accuses California of Trading Romance for Finance,"  January 2, 1926.

Upper left side of page has portrait of an older Ina Donna Coolbrith.  Lower left side of page has poem "A New Leaf" and handwritten note by Ms. Coolbrith.  Right side of page is a newspaper article on Ms.Coolbrith's thoughts on romance, art, and California.

Another photo is this series shows the address as "11979 San Vincente Blvd". However, the address of the building appears to have changed and is now "11950 San Vincente Blvd".

description_tesim: This image is of Teresa Kirbe, an actress who played the Queen of Paflagonia, in the opera "The Rose and the Ring", as mentioned by the February 25, 1957 articles in the Los Angeles Examiner, the Los Angeles Herald and Express, and the San Diego Tribune. She wears pearl earrings, a pearl necklace, a scoop neck and a swept up hairdo.  Her eyes are watering, a single tear runs down her right cheek.

title_tesim: "Hansel and Gretel" production with Barbara Ludwig and Charlie Marie Gordon (possibly), Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1959?
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 1957-59. Hansel & Gretel
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.

description_tesim: There are two related performance photographs and a third photo featuring Barbara Ludwig and the singer who portrays Hansel and Gretel’s father (dated 1959): 21198/zz002d433v, 21198/zz002d45zr and 21198/zz002d438f.

Possibly related to a production of "Hansel and Gretel," January 23-24, 1959. The performance was conducted by Irving Beckman and performed at Barnum Hall. The notice appears in the Los Angeles Times, 18 Jan. 1959: E6

Barbara Ludwig as Gretel and (possibly) Charlie Marie Gordon as Hansel, perform center stage in a Santa Monica Civic Opera production of "Hansel and Gretel." The singers stand within a cottage interior set with a fireplace and stone chimney, a curtained window and door. Three brooms are propped against the wall.  A table with stools, bowls, and other knick-knacks complete the set. Balcony view of stage and orchestra pit. Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.
